title: Protocol for subcutaneous implantation of polyethylene tubes containing endodontic reparative materials in 12-week-old Wistar rats
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"rpf6m","text":"The subcutaneous implantation of filled polyethylene tubes is traditionally used to investigate local biological responses to a material placed within this type of tube. An extensive report regarding this implantation technique in the 1960’s validated this type of implantation since their results indicated that “after 60 days of implantation the tubes were surrounded by a noninflamed connective tissue capsule”. Thus, a potential tissue reaction (i.e., inflammation) to an implanted filled tube could be attributed to the material/substance within the tube. However, it is crucial to include in the experimental setup empty tubes to be implanted similarly, serving as controls. Here, we describe a protocol for tube implantation used to investigate in vivo the tissue response to implanted polyethylene tubes containing calcium silicate-based materials. The expected results following these implantations include qualitative histological analysis and, additionally, quantitative data from score analysis are also obtainable.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":752,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cwnl6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"cgue6","text":"Subcutaneous implantation of filled polyethylene tubes using is traditionally used to investigate biological local responses to a material placed within this type of tube. An extensive report1 regarding this implantation technique in the 1960’s validated this type of implantation since their results indicated that ‘after 60 days of implantation the tubes were surrounded by a noninflamed connective tissue capsule’. Thus, a potential tissue reaction to an implanted filled tube could be attributed to the material/substance within the tube. Besides, it is crucial to include in the experimental setup empty tubes to be implanted similarly serving as controls. The observation of the Animal Research: Reporting of In Vivo Experiments (ARRIVE) guidelines2 (https://arriveguidelines.org) is crucial at all times since their ‘Essential 10’ steps and relevant examples include all the aspects necessary to conduct and report adequately replicable animal research. Besides, other guidelines are useful for reporting such as the PRIASE 2021 guidelines for reporting animal studies in Endodontology3. Additionally, it is highly recommended to observe the 3Rs4 principles when selecting the animal model and the number of materials and/or compositions to be tested using animals, especially considering that preliminary in vitro tests are mandatory to validate potential meaningful compositions that qualify to be tested in vivo.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":1313,"length":8},{"style":"italic","offset":1414,"length":7},{"style":"sup","offset":191,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":754,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":1092,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":1152,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":757,"length":28}],"data":{}},{"key":"nz3hj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dnw5g","text":"Biomaterial is defined as: “a substance that has been designed to assume a form that, alone or as part of a complex system, is used to direct, by controlling interactions with components of living systems, the course of any therapeutic or diagnostic procedure”5. The term 'biocompatible' has been used in association with biomaterials; however, biocompatibility must be considered as a complex system where all biological variable aspects of the host are considered along with the material properties, and the interaction between them6,7. For these reasons, animal models are highly useful for testing the materials effects since their higher biological complexity – compared to cell culture assays for example – provide a larger range of analytical possibilities.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":260,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":534,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mwgu6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f8dvx","text":"In endodontics, calcium silicate-based compositions have been used for decades after its development8 and the first patent registration9. These cements are hydraulic, thus after hydration the material hardens as a result of its hydration reaction, precipitating calcium hydroxide (portlandite) on its surface10 and calcium ions release in a decreasing concentration11,12. Furthermore, local alkalinity has been reported due to the release of these calcium ions and also hydroxyl ions13,14. These materials have been widely used and its use has been reported in clinical trials for vital pulp treatments in paediatric dentistry15,16 and also in studies considering the permanent dentition17–20. Furthermore, other reports of clinical use of this material in patients report the sealing of perforations21, apexification22, surgical procedures23–27, and also as a cervical barrier in revascularization procedures28,29. The favourable clinical performance reported for this material can be associated with its chemical release properties, the induction of local repair and the local alkaline pH provided by this material, and mainly by its ability to set in a moist environment. However, the mechanisms on how these different materials compositions (containing different additives30) behave biologically in an in vivo scenario justify the application of the proposed protocol of subcutaneous implantation of polyethylene tubes containing endodontic reparative materials in 12-week-old Wistar rats.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":1306,"length":7},{"style":"sup","offset":100,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":135,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":308,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":365,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":483,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":626,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":687,"length":7},{"style":"sup","offset":800,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":817,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":840,"length":7},{"style":"sup","offset":909,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":1276,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"y3vid","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2acys","text":"The project must be submitted a priori for analysis by the Animal Research Ethics Committee. The sample size calculation can be carried out using the Gpower 3.0 software31 considering the power of 0.80, a significance level of 5% for a medium effect size32.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":30,"length":8},{"style":"sup","offset":169,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":254,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fnlh1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ef0n5","text":"Depending on the study design or the condition tested, a sham group can be considered to be included33–35.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":100,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gw9zd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"87082F1DEF7411EFB0B30A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://arriveguidelines.org/arrive-guidelines/study-design"}}}}

steps: Obtain Wistar rats (12-week-old is here exemplified)
Perform a unique animal codification (1, 2, 3…)

Mirror the codification in a spreadsheet column and highlight the relevant experimental group division by material(s) to be implanted and time point(s) (to be printed and affixed in the surgical room for guidance)
Acquire the selected material(s) for the in vivo implantation after the in vitro validation

Chemically characterise the material before implantation using preferably different analytical techniques 14,36 to double-check the material chemical composition stated by the manufacturer. It is utterly encouraged to report the material(s) batch(es)

Stablish in vitro replicable material handling and manipulation (including material flow and setting time) in advance (in a sufficient number of samples that allow replicability) by either following the manufacturer's instructions or a relevant ISO standard 37 for the material testing

Polyethylene tubes of 1.0 mm internal diameter, 1.6 mm external diameter and 10.0 mm length can be used 36 – this tube configuration can be adjusted according to the study design following the instructions of the relevant ISO standard 38 that suggest: ‘Non-solid materials (including powders) may be contained in open-ended cylindrical tubes for the purpose of testing for local effects after implantation’ and ‘test samples shall be 1,5 mm to 2 mm in diameter, 5 mm to 10 mm in length and have rounded ends’
Sterilise the tubes using ethylene oxide according to the relevant ISO standard (ISO 10993-7:2008 Biological evaluation of medical devices Part 7: Ethylene oxide sterilization residuals)

Run all the following steps in a dedicated private room with low light, clean floor, with disinfected surfaces/covered by a surgical sterile cloth and isolated from noise. It is crucial to perform the experiment at one animal at a time, to avoid stress
Veterinary scale using the 'live animal' mode (i.e., Mars AD500S digital scale, São Paulo, SP) for the weight assessments
Run normality test (i.e., Shapiro Wilk) to evaluate the data distribution (a normal distribution is desired)

Separate one animal at a time to perform intramuscular anaesthesia with a combination of ketamine (Dopalen®) (80 mg/kg) and xylazine (Anasedan®) (10 mg/kg). Immobilisation should be performed using a clean cloth covering the animal vision ( Figure 1 )

After approximately five minutes of anaesthesia, test the animal's responsiveness by gentle digital compression of the paw. If the animal is still responsive, the anaesthesia can be supplemented with 1/4 of the initial dose, followed by three minutes wait prior to the second responsiveness test.
Perform trichotomy of the dorsal region gently in a squared format (~5 x 5 cm) using blades or electric shaving machines ( Figure 2 )
Perform antisepsis of the trichotomized area with povidone-iodine (PVPI) ( Figure 2 )
Hold the tissue up to avoid a deep blade perforation. This step can be performed using either the opposite hand or with a delicate Lane tissue forceps
Make a continuous longitudinal incision in the mid-dorsal region using a #15 scalpel ( Figure 2 )
Perform the divulsion of the subcutaneous tissue with blunt-tip scissors bilaterally along the animal flanks for the insertion of the filled and control tubes ( Figure 2 )
When 4 tubes are to be implanted, we strongly recommend that two separate smaller incisions are performed, and one tube can be placed in each side ( Figure 3 )
Hydrate the calcium silicate-based materials according to the manufacturer's instructions or to prior in vitro validation observing steps 4, 5 and 6
Considering that 4 tubes will be implanted in each animal, fill 3 sterile tubes with the freshly mixed material and leave 1 tube empty, for control
Place one filled tube in positions ‘a’, ‘b’ and ‘c’ and the empty tube in position ‘d’ for control ( Figure 3 )

Make sure that the tubes are in direct contact with the exposed connective tissue and sufficiently far from the incision – into the animal flank – to prevent their expulsion during the animal's mobility
Perform suture with 4.0 silk thread (i.e., Ethilon, Johnson & Johnson, USA) with four or five suture points for the continuous incision or two to three suture points for the smaller incision

Identify animals grouping by material in identified cages. Never mix animals with different implanted materials to avoid any contamination through contact, saliva, urine, faeces and/or cross-contamination by the food provided
Administer dipyrone at a dosage of 100 mg/kg on the opposite paw used for the anaesthesia ( Figure 4 )

Evaluate the animal recover daily considering any signs of distress. If animals present extended signs of discomfort, consider anticipated euthanasia (and exclusion from the study) with the consultation of animal research facility veterinarian
Keep, for five to seven days, the animals in individual cages for recovery
An initial hair growth can be observed in the trichotomy region after this period
Group the animals according to the implanted material

Provide a controlled room temperature (20 ± 1°C) during the experimental period with an automatic 12/12-hour light cycle (lights on from 8 am to 8 pm) and a relative humidity of ~55%. The normal temperature range in rats is between 35.9 and 37.5°C 39 ; this physiological parameter can be used during postoperative care after the tube implantations
Provide a balanced commercial solid food ad libitum
Provide filtered water ad libitum
Sanitize the cages by replacing the animals into a new cage containing ‘fresh’ bedding (wood shavings) periodically (every 2 to 3 days) throughout the experimental period
The animal needs to be deeply anesthetized (assessing again its weight) with a tripled dose, also intramuscularly

At this point, intracardiac blood sampling (3 to 5 ml for each animal) can be performed using a 25.06 needle and a 5 ml syringe after ventral full access, if planned 40 . Store the blood sample in a vial with an anticoagulant for erythrograms and leukograms (purple lidded vial) or biochemical analysis light-protected (red lidded vial) ( Figure 5 )
The euthanasia can be confirmed by observing the absence of respiratory movement (apnoea) and the loss of the corneal reflex
Locate the implanted tube area by palpation and incise and dissect the tissue. Make sure to cover sufficient surrounding normal tissue of at least 5 mm to each tube side

Immerse the tissue segments containing the tube for fixation in a 10% buffered formalin at neutral pH, in individual identified (using the unique code described in steps 2 and 3). It is highly recommended to have all the necessary individual flasks identified and filled with the 10% buffered formalin to at least ten-fold the sample volume (5 to 8 ml, for these shown samples) previously to the proceedings. Do not perform the euthanasia without the flasks ready to be used
The tissues in the flasks containing 10% buffered formalin can be stored at room temperature. A minimum of 24 to 48 hours for tissue fixation is required
(Briefly described here, and adjustable to the availability of equipment) For the histological processing the samples must be washed in running water for 12 hours
The excess adjacent tissue can be eliminated manually using microtome blades, leaving the sample with a rectangular shape and tissue surrounding the implant, sufficient to carry out the processing
The tubes can be now carefully separated from the fixated tissue and the tissue allocated separately in histological cassettes for a brief second fixation ( Figure 6 )
Washed in running water overnight

Sample dehydration is accomplished by immersion in graded steps of ethanol (50%, 70% and 100%) for dehydration for 1.5 hours for each concentration. CRUCIAL STEP: Additionally, two extra 1.5-hour immersion cycles must be performed using the 100% ethanol
Immerse samples in 1:1 proportion of 100% ethanol and xylene P.A. for 12 hours
Immerse samples in fresh xylene P.A. for three cycles of 1.5 hours
Paraffin embedding (i.e., Histosec® or Paraplast®) – suggested to liquefy at 62°C and immerse samples twice for 1 hour
Place the sample in aluminium moulds for cooling

The paraffin blocks can now be subjected to semi-serial microtomy using a microtome in 5 µm sections and its placement over glass slides. CRUCIAL STEP: make sure that the selected slices are obtained in longitudinal direction of the tubular tissue
Perform routine haematoxylin and eosin staining (H&E)

a. Haematoxylin (Gills III) i. Reagents (for ~1 L of solution) Ethylene glycol – 250 ml Distilled water – 750 ml Haematoxylin – 6 g Sodium Iodate – 0.6 g Aluminium sulphate – 80 g Glacial acetic acid – 20 ml ii. Dissolve ethylene glycol in distilled water iii. Add haematoxylin crystals iv. Add sodium iodate v. Add acetic acid vi. Add chloral hydrate – agitate gently until fully homogeneous (~15 min). If prepared correctly, the solution will present a strong red colour and in contact with tap water will become blue

b. Eosin Y stock solution: i. Reagents (for~ 200 ml of stock solution) Eosin Y – 2 g Distilled water – 40 ml 95% ethanol – 160 ml ii. Dissolve Eosin Y in water, mix until dissolved iii. Add ethanol and mix
c. Eosin Y working solution: i. Reagents (for ~800 ml of working solution) Eosin stock solution – 200ml 70% ethanol – 200 ml Glacial acetic acid – 4 ml ii. Dilute eosin stock solution in 70% ethanol
d. Other required solutions: i. 0.3% acetic acid in distilled water ii. 0.3% hydrochloric acid in 70% ethanol iii. Scott’s tap water substitute

e. H&E staining 41, 42 - place slides in a suitable holder, adding enough of the following solutions to ensure slides are covered: i. Dewaxing: Xylene – 5 min (twice) ii. Sample rehydration: 100% ethanol – 1 minute (twice) 95% ethanol – 1 minute 70% ethanol – 1 minute Tap water – 2 minutes iii. Staining: Haematoxylin (Gills) – 4 mins Running tap water – 2 minutes (until water runs clear) Differentiate with 0.3% acetic acid – 30 seconds Drain Treat with 0.3% hydrochloric acid in 70% ethanol – 30 seconds Running tap water – 2 minutes Treat with Scott’s tap water substitute – 2 mins Running tap water – 2 mins Eosin – 1 min Running tap water – 2 minutes (until water runs clear) Drain well iv. Dehydration: 100% ethanol – 1 min (twice) Xylene – 1 min (twice)
f. Canada balsam or DPX for permanent mounting of slides for microscopy, including a coverslip.
Immunohistochemistry techniques can also be performed after these described steps according to different protocols 43 and markers of interest can be selected to stain the obtained sections

The number of inflammatory cells can be counted for each specimen image (i.e., n = 35 slides for each material/time point or control/time at 40× magnification, as previously reported by our research group 36 ) by a calibrated operator ideally blinded to the image condition, using a light microscopy (i.e., DM 4000 B; Leica Microsystem, Wetzlar, Germany)
Acquire and export images in RAW, TIFF or other uncompressed format (compressed image formats are not suitable)
Scale bars can be positioned within the images using ImageJ software ( https://imagej.net/ij/download.html ) after calibration (adjustable for each microscopy and image acquisition system)
The time taken for each step (when relevant) is informed within the 'procedure' section

Weight It is expected that the animals present a gradual weight gain as a consequence of the normal growth and development, despite the implantation procedures. Here we present the weight data from 12-week-old animals subjected to the tube implantation and euthanized in two time points ( Figure 7 ) after 4 and 25 weeks. This data indicates a significant gradual weight gain comparable with non-implanted animals kept under the same conditions. Histological images (H&E) The region of interest (ROI) for imaging following implantation is the adjacent tissue to the two tips of the implanted filled tube allowing the tissue reaction comparison between different materials with the unfilled control tubes. Histological images are obtainable from this ROI of the tissue in different magnifications. In Figure 8 , examples of images are shown at different magnifications indicating the tissue shape from where the tube was removed (in ‘A’ smaller magnification) allowing to identify its tip. Detailed images from the tissue adjacent to the tube tip (in ‘B’ higher magnification) allow to visualize the cells and, potentially, attribute scores to obtain quantitative data from histological images of the same material in different biological replicates indicating, for example, the number of inflammatory cells at different time points 36 .

Biocompatibility analysis in subcutaneous tissue and physico-chemical analysis of pre-mixed calcium silicate–based sealers by Ana Cristina Padilha Janini, Lauter Eston Pelepenko, Juliana Minto Boldieri, Victor Augusto Benedicto dos Santos, Nilvan Alves da Silva, Ivo Milton Raimundo, Brenda P. F. A. Gomes, Marina Angélica Marciano published in Clinical Oral Investigations on 28 Mar, 2023 https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s00784-023-04957-9

Figure 1. Intramuscular anaesthesia with a combination of ketamine (80 mg/kg) and xylazine (10 mg/kg) and immobilisation performed using a clean cloth covering the animal vision Figure 2. Trichotomy of the dorsal region, antisepsis of the trichotomized area with povidone-iodine (PVPI), continuous longitudinal incision in the mid-dorsal region using a #15 scalpel and divulsion of the subcutaneous tissue with blunt-tip scissors bilaterally Figure 3. Suggested separate incision for the implantation of 4 tubes Figure 4. Pos-operative Dipyrone at a dosage of 100 mg/kg Figure 5. Intracardiac blood sampling (3 to 5 ml for each animal) can be performed using a 25.06 needle and a 5 ml syringe after ventral full access, if necessary. Store the blood sample in a vial with an anticoagulant for erythrograms and leukograms (purple lidded vial) or biochemical analysis light-protected (red lidded vial) Figure 6. Tubes carefully separated from the fixated tissue and the tissue allocated separately in histological cassettes for a brief second overnight fixation Figure 7. Analysis of (n = 44) 12-week-old animals’ weight subject to tube implantations, maintenance and euthanasia (after 4 and 25 weeks [n = 22 each]) as described in this protocol. Blue dots indicate negative control animals without any sham or implantation procedure. A gradual significant weight gain is observed throughout the experimental period. It is crucial to initially include animals with significantly similar weight (as represented in the Normal QQ plot highlight, using Shapiro Wilk test) allowing future comparison between the time points. One-way ANOVA and Tukey tests were used to stablish comparisons of this data Figure 8. Non-related examples of representative images at different magnifications indicating the tissue shape from where the tube was removed (in ‘A’ smaller magnification) allowing to identify its end tip. Detailed images from the tissue adjacent to the tube tip (in ‘B’ higher magnification) allow to navigate and visualize the cells and, potentially, attribute scores and obtain quantitative data from histological images of the same material in different biological replicates
